abstract:
Image Matching is a core component of all best-performing algorithms and
pipelines in 3D vision. Yet despite matching being fundamentally a 3D problem,
intrinsically linked to camera pose and scene geometry, it is typically treated
as a 2D problem. This makes sense as the goal of matching is to establish
correspondences between 2D pixel fields, but also seems like a potentially
hazardous choice. In this work, we take a different stance and propose to cast
matching as a 3D task with DUSt3R, a recent and powerful 3D reconstruction
framework based on Transformers. Based on pointmaps regression, this method
displayed impressive robustness in matching views with extreme viewpoint
changes, yet with limited accuracy. We aim here to improve the matching
capabilities of such an approach while preserving its robustness. We thus
propose to augment the DUSt3R network with a new head that outputs dense local
features, trained with an additional matching loss. We further address the
issue of quadratic complexity of dense matching, which becomes prohibitively
slow for downstream applications if not carefully treated. We introduce a fast
reciprocal matching scheme that not only accelerates matching by orders of
magnitude, but also comes with theoretical guarantees and, lastly, yields
improved results. Extensive experiments show that our approach, coined MASt3R,
significantly outperforms the state of the art on multiple matching tasks. In
particular, it beats the best published methods by 30% (absolute improvement)
in VCRE AUC on the extremely challenging Map-free localization dataset.

keyInsights: Pixel correspondences fundamentally exist because they observe the same 3D point in a scene, and explicitly reasoning in 3D space improves matching performance, especially under extreme viewpoint changes.
A dedicated matching head can be integrated into an existing 3D reconstruction framework (DUSt3R) to jointly predict 3D pointmaps and dense local feature descriptors.

An efficient fast reciprocal matching algorithm, leveraging iterative subsampling and mutual nearest neighbor checks, can significantly reduce computational complexity for dense matching while maintaining or improving accuracy.
originalProblem: Traditional 2D image matching methods struggle to establish robust correspondences in challenging scenarios involving extreme viewpoint or illumination changes.

Existing deep learning-based image matching approaches (e.g., SuperGlue, LoFTR) operate primarily in the 2D domain, limiting their ability to fully leverage the inherent 3D nature of the correspondence problem.
Generating dense and accurate pixel correspondences while maintaining computational efficiency for practical applications remains a challenge.
results: MASt3R achieved a 30% absolute improvement in VCRE AUC on the Map-free localization benchmark compared to the previous state-of-the-art, significantly reducing median translation error.
The method set a new state-of-the-art on the CO3Dv2 and RealEstate10k datasets for relative pose estimation, demonstrating strong performance across diverse scene types.
The fast reciprocal matching algorithm achieved similar or better performance than exhaustive matching while being 64 times faster when using just 3,000 sampled points.
solution: NAVER LABS Europe developed MASt3R, an image matching framework that extends the DUSt3R architecture by integrating a dedicated matching head alongside its 3D pointmap prediction head.
The architecture employs a Siamese network with shared ViT encoders and cross-attention decoders to extract deep features and enable information exchange between image pairs.

An efficient fast reciprocal matching algorithm performs iterative subsampling and reciprocal nearest neighbor searches to extract reliable pixel correspondences from dense feature maps, optimizing computational cost.
paper_summary.summary: MASt3R, developed by NAVER LABS Europe, presents an image matching framework that explicitly incorporates 3D scene information to establish pixel correspondences. The method achieves a 30% absolute improvement in VCRE AUC on the map-free localization benchmark and sets new state-of-the-art results for relative pose estimation on CO3Dv2 and RealEstate10k datasets.

title: Grounding Image Matching in 3D with MASt3R

abstract: Structure-from-Motion (SfM), a task aiming at jointly recovering camera poses and 3D geometry of a scene given a set of images, remains a hard problem with still many open challenges despite decades of significant progress. The traditional solution for SfM consists of a complex pipeline of minimal solvers which tends to propagate errors and fails when images do not sufficiently overlap, have too little motion, etc. Recent methods have attempted to revisit this paradigm, but we empirically show that they fall short of fixing these core issues. In this paper, we propose instead to build upon a recently released foundation model for 3D vision that can robustly produce local 3D reconstructions and accurate matches. We introduce a low-memory approach to accurately align these local reconstructions in a global coordinate system. We further show that such foundation models can serve as efficient image retrievers without any overhead, reducing the overall complexity from quadratic to linear. Overall, our novel SfM pipeline is simple, scalable, fast and truly unconstrained, i.e. it can handle any collection of images, ordered or not. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks show that our method provides steady performance across diverse settings, especially outperforming existing methods in small- and medium-scale settings.

keyInsights: Leveraging foundation models for 3D vision, specifically MASt3R, enables robust pairwise geometry and camera parameter estimation without requiring camera motion.
A sparse scene graph constructed via image retrieval allows the system to scale quasi-linearly with the number of images by avoiding exhaustive pairwise matching.
Combining 3D matching loss for coarse alignment and 2D reprojection error with pseudo-tracks for refinement provides a robust global optimization strategy that avoids RANSAC.
originalProblem: Traditional Structure-from-Motion (SfM) pipelines, like COLMAP, rely on complex sequential steps that often struggle with challenging scenarios.
Existing SfM methods face difficulties with insufficient image overlap, lack of motion between views (e.g., pure rotation), and handling outliers.
Scalability remains a challenge for large image collections due to the quadratic complexity of exhaustive pairwise image matching.
results: MASt3R-SfM outperforms existing methods on small-scale settings (3-10 views), maintaining higher accuracy where others struggle.
The pipeline effectively handles unordered image collections with varying viewpoints and illumination changes.
MASt3R-SfM achieves comparable results to state-of-the-art methods on established benchmarks such as Tanks&Temples and ETH3D while offering improved robustness and scalability.
solution: MASt3R-SfM introduces a pipeline that leverages the MASt3R foundation model for 3D vision, integrating its local image pair geometry estimates into a coherent global reconstruction.
It constructs a sparse scene graph using image retrieval (ASMK) to connect likely-overlapping image pairs, reducing complexity to quasi-linear scaling.

The system performs local 3D reconstruction and sparse pixel matching for each graph edge using MASt3R, followed by a two-stage global optimization process involving 3D alignment and 2D reprojection error minimization with pseudo-tracks.
paper_summary.summary: MASt3R-SfM, from the authors at CentraleSupélec, presents a Structure-from-Motion pipeline that uses foundation models for 3D vision to reconstruct 3D scenes from unconstrained image collections. The system demonstrates improved robustness in challenging scenarios like limited overlap and pure rotation, while maintaining quasi-linear complexity with the number of images.

title: MASt3R-SfM: a Fully-Integrated Solution for Unconstrained Structure-from-Motion

abstract:
Multimodal remote sensing image (MRSI) matching is pivotal for cross-modal
fusion, localization, and object detection, but it faces severe challenges due
to geometric, radiometric, and viewpoint discrepancies across imaging
modalities. Existing unimodal datasets lack scale and diversity, limiting deep
learning solutions. This paper proposes MapGlue, a universal MRSI matching
framework, and MapData, a large-scale multimodal dataset addressing these gaps.
Our contributions are twofold. MapData, a globally diverse dataset spanning 233
sampling points, offers original images (7,000x5,000 to 20,000x15,000 pixels).
After rigorous cleaning, it provides 121,781 aligned electronic map-visible
image pairs (512x512 pixels) with hybrid manual-automated ground truth,
addressing the scarcity of scalable multimodal benchmarks. MapGlue integrates
semantic context with a dual graph-guided mechanism to extract cross-modal
invariant features. This structure enables global-to-local interaction,
enhancing descriptor robustness against modality-specific distortions.
Extensive evaluations on MapData and five public datasets demonstrate MapGlue's
superiority in matching accuracy under complex conditions, outperforming
state-of-the-art methods. Notably, MapGlue generalizes effectively to unseen
modalities without retraining, highlighting its adaptability. This work
addresses longstanding challenges in MRSI matching by combining scalable
dataset construction with a robust, semantics-driven framework. Furthermore,
MapGlue shows strong generalization capabilities on other modality matching
tasks for which it was not specifically trained. The dataset and code are
available at this https URL

keyInsights: Incorporating saliency maps helps guide keypoint extraction to salient regions
Fusing semantic information from MobileSAM with structural features improves matching robustness
Dual graph structure enables better feature interaction across modalities
Soft matching score matrix with dual-softmax improves reliability
originalProblem: Existing multimodal remote sensing image matching methods struggle with geometric distortions, radiometric differences, and cross-domain representation gaps
Lack of large-scale, high-quality benchmark datasets for training and evaluating matching algorithms
Limited capability to handle complex scene variations in scale, rotation, viewpoint and modality
results: 961.3% improvement in AUC@5px compared to best baseline on hard transformations
Strong generalization to other datasets including MultiResSAR-Low, SRIF, CoFSM without retraining
Effective performance on natural image datasets like DIODE and LGHD LWIR/RGB
Ablation studies confirm significant contributions from saliency enhancement and dual graph components
solution: Created MapData dataset with 233 geographic sampling points using Google Maps/Earth data and hybrid annotation
Developed MapGlue framework using saliency-enhanced SuperPoint detection and semantic feature fusion
Implemented dual graph structure with intra-image and inter-image connections for enhanced feature matching
paper_summary.summary: A large-scale multimodal remote sensing image matching framework combines saliency-enhanced feature detection with dual graph structures to achieve 961.3% improvement in matching accuracy on challenging transformations while generalizing effectively across multiple remote sensing and natural image datasets.

title: MapGlue: Multimodal Remote Sensing Image Matching

abstract:
Feature matching is an important computer vision task that involves
estimating correspondences between two images of a 3D scene, and dense methods
estimate all such correspondences. The aim is to learn a robust model, i.e., a
model able to match under challenging real-world changes. In this work, we
propose such a model, leveraging frozen pretrained features from the foundation
model DINOv2. Although these features are significantly more robust than local
features trained from scratch, they are inherently coarse. We therefore combine
them with specialized ConvNet fine features, creating a precisely localizable
feature pyramid. To further improve robustness, we propose a tailored
transformer match decoder that predicts anchor probabilities, which enables it
to express multimodality. Finally, we propose an improved loss formulation
through regression-by-classification with subsequent robust regression. We
conduct a comprehensive set of experiments that show that our method, RoMa,
achieves significant gains, setting a new state-of-the-art. In particular, we
achieve a 36% improvement on the extremely challenging WxBS benchmark. Code is
provided at https://github.com/Parskatt/RoMa

organization_info.name: East China University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Features optimized for robust coarse matching (e.g., DINOv2) differ from those best for precise fine localization (e.g., specialized ConvNets), necessitating a decoupled feature pyramid.
Representing coarse matches via regression-by-classification allows modeling multimodal matching distributions, which is important for handling ambiguities.
A scale-aware loss formulation that uses KL divergence for coarse predictions and robust regression for fine refinements improves accuracy and robustness.
originalProblem: Current dense feature matching methods struggle with robustness and generalization to significant real-world variations due to overfitting on limited 3D-supervised datasets.
While powerful, general-purpose foundation model features are inherently coarse and lack the fine-grained precision required for accurate dense matching.
Existing match decoding and loss formulations do not adequately address the multimodal nature of coarse matches or robustly handle outliers during refinement.
results: Achieves a 36% improvement in mean Average Accuracy (mAA) on the challenging WxBS benchmark (80.1 mAA vs. DKM's 58.9 mAA), demonstrating superior robustness.
Reduces the error on the IMC2022 benchmark by 26% relative to the previous best, reaching 88.0 mAA.
Sets new state-of-the-art AUC scores on MegaDepth and ScanNet pose estimation, becoming the first method to exceed 70% AUC@20° on ScanNet-1500 (70.9%).
solution: Uses a "feature specialization" strategy with frozen DINOv2 for robust coarse features and a specialized ConvNet for fine, precisely localizable features.
Introduces a Transformer-based match decoder that predicts anchor probabilities (regression-by-classification) to model multimodal distributions at coarse scales.
Applies a robust, scale-aware loss formulation: KL divergence for coarse (regression-by-classification) and a robust regression loss (generalized Charbonnier) for fine refinement.
paper_summary.summary: RoMa introduces a robust dense feature matching model that combines frozen DINOv2 features for coarse matching with specialized ConvNet features for fine localization, alongside a Transformer decoder and a scale-aware loss formulation. The approach achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks, including a 36% improvement in mean Average Accuracy on the WxBS benchmark for robustness.

title: RoMa: Robust Dense Feature Matching

abstract: Dense feature matching aims to estimate all correspondences between two images of a 3D scene and has recently been established as the gold-standard due to its high accuracy and robustness. However, existing dense matchers still fail or perform poorly for many hard real-world scenarios, and high-precision models are often slow, limiting their applicability. In this paper, we attack these weaknesses on a wide front through a series of systematic improvements that together yield a significantly better model. In particular, we construct a novel matching architecture and loss, which, combined with a curated diverse training distribution, enables our model to solve many complex matching tasks. We further make training faster through a decoupled two-stage matching-then-refinement pipeline, and at the same time, significantly reduce refinement memory usage through a custom CUDA kernel. Finally, we leverage the recent DINOv3 foundation model along with multiple other insights to make the model more robust and unbiased. In our extensive set of experiments we show that the resulting novel matcher sets a new state-of-the-art, being significantly more accurate than its predecessors. Code is available at this https URL

organization_info.name: Centre for Mathematical Sciences, Lund University

keyInsights: Upgrading to DINOv3 features while keeping the backbone frozen effectively maintains robustness to appearance changes while significantly enhancing overall feature quality and matching performance.
Incorporating explicit multi-view context within the coarse matcher, alongside a robust regression loss, substantially improves the model's ability to handle challenging geometric transformations.
Predicting pixel-wise error covariance provides valuable, actionable uncertainty estimates that, when leveraged, dramatically improve downstream 3D pose estimation reliability.
originalProblem: Existing dense matchers struggle with complex real-world conditions, including extreme viewpoint changes, fine-grained object matching, and texture-poor surfaces.
Prior state-of-the-art dense matching models, like the original RoMa, lacked true multi-view context in their coarse matcher, limiting performance under extreme geometric changes.
High-precision dense matching models often suffer from significant computational bottlenecks (slow runtime, high memory consumption), hindering their widespread application.
results: Achieves new state-of-the-art in relative pose estimation on MegaDepth-1500 and ScanNet-1500, outperforming all prior matchers and feed-forward 3D reconstruction methods.
Runs 1.7 times faster than the original RoMa, achieving 30.9 pairs/s on 640x640 images, while maintaining a similar memory footprint (4.8 GB) due to architectural optimizations.

Demonstrates superior dense matching performance across six diverse datasets, including an 84% lower End-Point Error on AerialMegaDepth compared to RoMa, and stronger generalization to multi-modal and extreme-scale datasets like SatAst.
solution: Employs DINOv3 as a frozen feature extractor backbone and introduces a multi-view Transformer with an auxiliary negative log-likelihood loss for improved coarse matching.
Streamlines the refinement architecture with a custom CUDA kernel for local correlation and optimized channel dimensions to enhance efficiency and reduce memory footprint.

Integrates pixel-wise predictive error covariance with a dedicated training loss, providing quantifiable uncertainty estimates for downstream tasks, and trains on a significantly more diverse dataset mixture.
paper_summary.summary: RoMa v2 introduces a dense feature matching model that establishes new state-of-the-art accuracy and robustness across diverse benchmarks by leveraging DINOv3 features and novel multi-view context. The model achieves 1.7 times faster throughput than its predecessor while maintaining a similar memory footprint and also predicts pixel-wise error covariances.

title: RoMa v2: Harder Better Faster Denser Feature Matching

abstract: Recovering 3D structure and camera motion from images has been a long-standing focus of computer vision research and is known as Structure-from-Motion (SfM). Solutions to this problem are categorized into incremental and global approaches. Until now, the most popular systems follow the incremental paradigm due to its superior accuracy and robustness, while global approaches are drastically more scalable and efficient. With this work, we revisit the problem of global SfM and propose GLOMAP as a new general-purpose system that outperforms the state of the art in global SfM. In terms of accuracy and robustness, we achieve results on-par or superior to COLMAP, the most widely used incremental SfM, while being orders of magnitude faster. We share our system as an open-source implementation at {this https URL}.

keyInsights: Jointly optimizing camera and 3D point positions through a points-only objective function is more robust and accurate than traditional separate translation averaging and triangulation.
The proposed global positioning approach is highly robust to noisy image observations, uncalibrated camera intrinsics, and challenging co-linear camera motions, improving generalizability.
By avoiding explicit relative translation constraints, GLOMAP bypasses the ill-posed nature of traditional global SfM, leading to better convergence and overall performance.
originalProblem: Incremental SfM methods, while accurate, suffer from poor scalability and high computational costs for large image collections due to repeated bundle adjustments.
Traditional global SfM methods are efficient but historically lack the accuracy and robustness of incremental approaches, primarily due to difficulties in the translation averaging step.
Existing global translation averaging methods struggle with scale ambiguity, sensitivity to inaccurate camera intrinsics, and degenerate co-linear camera motions.
results: GLOMAP achieved approximately 8% higher recall and 9-11 additional AUC points on ETH3D SLAM datasets compared to COLMAP, while being orders of magnitude faster.
On challenging uncalibrated internet photo datasets (IMC 2023), GLOMAP delivered about 4 points higher AUC scores and was approximately 8 times faster than COLMAP.
Reconstructions from GLOMAP produced similar PSNR and SSIM scores to COLMAP when used for novel view synthesis, demonstrating comparable output quality for downstream applications.
solution: GLOMAP introduces a novel "global positioning" step that jointly optimizes both camera and 3D point positions, effectively discarding explicit translation averaging.
The method formulates an objective function based on normalized camera ray differences, which is initialization-free and inherently robust to outliers.
It incorporates robust feature tracking, rotation averaging, and a final global bundle adjustment, along with camera clustering for cleaning up large, unordered internet photo collections.
paper_summary.summary: GLOMAP, a new global Structure-from-Motion (SfM) system, delivers state-of-the-art accuracy and robustness for 3D reconstruction from images, matching or exceeding leading incremental SfM systems like COLMAP while achieving significantly faster processing speeds. Its novel global positioning approach jointly estimates camera and 3D point positions, overcoming traditional global SfM limitations.

title: Global Structure-from-Motion Revisited

abstract: Matching local geometric features on real-world depth images is a challenging task due to the noisy, low-resolution, and incomplete nature of 3D scan data. These difficulties limit the performance of current state-of-art methods, which are typically based on histograms over geometric properties. In this paper, we present 3DMatch, a data-driven model that learns a local volumetric patch descriptor for establishing correspondences between partial 3D data. To amass training data for our model, we propose a self-supervised feature learning method that leverages the millions of correspondence labels found in existing RGB-D reconstructions. Experiments show that our descriptor is not only able to match local geometry in new scenes for reconstruction, but also generalize to different tasks and spatial scales (e.g. instance-level object model alignment for the Amazon Picking Challenge, and mesh surface correspondence). Results show that 3DMatch consistently outperforms other state-of-the-art approaches by a significant margin. Code, data, benchmarks, and pre-trained models are available online at this http URL

keyInsights: Existing high-fidelity RGB-D scene reconstructions can be leveraged as a vast, implicit source of ground truth correspondence labels for self-supervised learning.
3D volumetric representations, particularly TDF voxel grids processed by 3D ConvNets, are more effective than 2D approaches for robustly handling real-world 3D scan imperfections and spatial context.
Learned geometric descriptors can substantially outperform hand-crafted methods by implicitly capturing complex patterns and generalizing better across varied conditions.
originalProblem: Traditional hand-crafted 3D geometric descriptors struggle with noise, low resolution, and incompleteness inherent in real-world 3D scan data.
Manually annotating ground truth correspondences for local 3D patches is impractical for training large-scale deep learning models.
A need exists for a robust 3D feature matching method that generalizes across diverse tasks and spatial scales.
results: Achieved a 35.3% error rate (FPR at 95% recall) in keypoint matching, significantly outperforming hand-crafted descriptors like FPFH (61.3% error).
Improved geometric registration, yielding 65.1% recall and 25.2% precision on synthetic benchmarks, and 66.8% recall and 40.1% precision on real-world data.

Demonstrated strong generalization to new domains, outperforming baselines in 6D object pose estimation (61.0% correct rotation) and successfully finding surface correspondences on 3D meshes without fine-tuning.
solution: Developed a 3D Convolutional Neural Network (ConvNet) to learn a 512-dimensional local volumetric patch descriptor.
Implemented a self-supervised training strategy that automatically extracts over 8 million correspondence labels from existing RGB-D scene reconstructions.
Represented local 3D patches as 30x30x30 Truncated Distance Function (TDF) voxel grids, which are robust to partiality and noise.
paper_summary.summary: The research presents 3DMatch, a learned local volumetric patch descriptor, designed to establish accurate correspondences in real-world, noisy 3D scan data. This descriptor achieves a 35.3% error rate in keypoint matching, significantly outperforming traditional methods, and demonstrates strong generalization across various 3D tasks like object pose estimation and scene registration.

title: 3DMatch: Learning Local Geometric Descriptors from RGB-D Reconstructions

abstract:
We introduce LightGlue, a deep neural network that learns to match local
features across images. We revisit multiple design decisions of SuperGlue, the
state of the art in sparse matching, and derive simple but effective
improvements. Cumulatively, they make LightGlue more efficient - in terms of
both memory and computation, more accurate, and much easier to train. One key
property is that LightGlue is adaptive to the difficulty of the problem: the
inference is much faster on image pairs that are intuitively easy to match, for
example because of a larger visual overlap or limited appearance change. This
opens up exciting prospects for deploying deep matchers in latency-sensitive
applications like 3D reconstruction. The code and trained models are publicly
available at https://github.com/cvg/LightGlue.

keyInsights: Dynamically adjusting computational effort, through adaptive depth and width, in feature matching networks can yield substantial efficiency gains while preserving accuracy.
Replacing complex optimal transport layers with more efficient prediction heads and utilizing relative positional encoding improves model performance and reduces overhead.
A streamlined two-stage training process enables faster convergence and reduces the computational resources required for deep feature matching model development.
originalProblem: State-of-the-art deep feature matching methods, like SuperGlue, are computationally expensive, hindering real-time applications and deployment on resource-constrained devices.
SuperGlue processes all features with a fixed computational budget and relies on a complex Sinkhorn layer, leading to high inference and training costs.
The significant resource requirements limit the practical adoption of advanced feature matching in critical computer vision applications.
results: Achieves up to 10x faster inference speeds compared to SuperGlue, with lower memory requirements and improved scaling with increasing keypoint counts.
Demonstrates comparable or superior accuracy to SuperGlue on standard benchmarks, including state-of-the-art performance on relative pose estimation tasks.
Exhibits robust performance across various feature detectors (e.g., SIFT, SuperPoint, DISK) and diverse scene types, including outdoor and indoor environments.
solution: Implements relative positional encoding and a lightweight prediction head to reduce computational complexity compared to prior methods.
Introduces adaptive computation mechanisms, including early stopping and progressive point pruning, to dynamically adjust the network's depth and width based on matching task difficulty.
Develops a simplified two-stage training pipeline that converges faster and requires fewer computational resources.
paper_summary.summary: LightGlue presents an adaptive deep feature matching approach that achieves comparable or superior accuracy to SuperGlue while being up to 10x faster. It integrates adaptive computation mechanisms, such as early stopping and feature pruning, to dynamically adjust computational effort based on task complexity.

title: LightGlue: Local Feature Matching at Light Speed

abstract:
Local feature matching enjoys wide-ranging applications in the realm of
computer vision, encompassing domains such as image retrieval, 3D
reconstruction, and object recognition. However, challenges persist in
improving the accuracy and robustness of matching due to factors like viewpoint
and lighting variations. In recent years, the introduction of deep learning
models has sparked widespread exploration into local feature matching
techniques. The objective of this endeavor is to furnish a comprehensive
overview of local feature matching methods. These methods are categorized into
two key segments based on the presence of detectors. The Detector-based
category encompasses models inclusive of Detect-then-Describe, Joint Detection
and Description, Describe-then-Detect, as well as Graph Based techniques. In
contrast, the Detector-free category comprises CNN Based, Transformer Based,
and Patch Based methods. Our study extends beyond methodological analysis,
incorporating evaluations of prevalent datasets and metrics to facilitate a
quantitative comparison of state-of-the-art techniques. The paper also explores
the practical application of local feature matching in diverse domains such as
Structure from Motion, Remote Sensing Image Registration, and Medical Image
Registration, underscoring its versatility and significance across various
fields. Ultimately, we endeavor to outline the current challenges faced in this
domain and furnish future research directions, thereby serving as a reference
for researchers involved in local feature matching and its interconnected
domains. A comprehensive list of studies in this survey is available at
https://github.com/vignywang/Awesome-Local-Feature-Matching .

organization_info.name: Beijing University of Posts and Telecommunications

title: Local Feature Matching Using Deep Learning: A Survey

abstract: Segment matching is an important intermediate task in computer vision that establishes correspondences between semantically or geometrically coherent regions across images. Unlike keypoint matching, which focuses on localized features, segment matching captures structured regions, offering greater robustness to occlusions, lighting variations, and viewpoint changes. In this paper, we leverage the spatial understanding of 3D foundation models to tackle wide-baseline segment matching, a challenging setting involving extreme viewpoint shifts. We propose an architecture that uses the inductive bias of these 3D foundation models to match segments across image pairs with up to 180 degree view-point change rotation. Extensive experiments show that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods, including the SAM2 video propagator and local feature matching methods, by up to 30% on the AUPRC metric, on ScanNet++ and Replica datasets. We further demonstrate benefits of the proposed model on relevant downstream tasks, including 3D instance mapping and object-relative navigation. Project Page: this https URL

keyInsights: The inherent 3D inductive biases and cross-view interaction capabilities of 3D Foundation Models provide a powerful foundation for establishing geometrically consistent segment correspondences.
Transforming dense, geometry-aware patch-level features into robust segment descriptors via a lightweight adapter enables accurate instance-level matching even under severe viewpoint changes.
The combination of a learnable dustbin and Sinkhorn normalization effectively handles partial overlaps and unmatched segments, which is critical for wide-baseline scenarios.
originalProblem: Existing segment matching methods, predominantly reliant on 2D supervision, struggle with wide-baseline scenarios involving drastic viewpoint changes and large rotations (up to 180 degrees).
Current local feature matching (LFM) techniques and direct segment matching approaches often fail to maintain geometric consistency under profound transformations.
The lack of robust segment-level correspondences hinders advanced computer vision tasks such as 3D instance mapping, long-term object tracking, and object-relative robotic navigation.
results: Outperformed state-of-the-art baselines on ScanNet++ by up to 30% in AUPRC, particularly in the most challenging 135°-180° pose bin (83.6% AUPRC for SegMASt3R vs. 45.4% for the next best LFM baseline).
Demonstrated strong generalization to novel indoor (Replica) and outdoor (MapFree) scenes, showing robustness to noisy segmentation masks generated by FastSAM.

Achieved higher Average Precision in 3D instance mapping (e.g., 79.93 AP on Replica 'office0' with GT masks vs. 43.53 AP for ConceptGraphs) and improved object-level topological navigation success (27% absolute SPL increase in hard settings).
solution: Utilizes a frozen MASt3R backbone, a 3D Foundation Model pre-trained on diverse 3D-vision tasks, to extract geometry-aware patch-level features from image pairs.
Introduces a lightweight 'Feat2Seg Adapter' (MLP head) that upsamples patch features to image resolution and aggregates them into segment-level descriptors using masked average pooling.

Employs a differentiable segment matching layer that computes cosine similarity between segment embeddings, integrates a learnable dustbin, and uses Sinkhorn normalization to derive robust, soft and then discrete correspondences.
paper_summary.summary: SegMASt3R introduces a geometry-grounded approach for wide-baseline segment matching by leveraging a frozen 3D Foundation Model backbone (MASt3R) combined with a specialized Feat2Seg Adapter and a differentiable matching layer. The method achieves up to a 30% improvement in AUPRC on ScanNet++ for extreme viewpoint changes and significantly enhances performance in 3D instance mapping and object-level topological navigation.

title: SegMASt3R: Geometry Grounded Segment Matching

abstract:
This paper introduces SuperGlue, a neural network that matches two sets of
local features by jointly finding correspondences and rejecting non-matchable
points. Assignments are estimated by solving a differentiable optimal transport
problem, whose costs are predicted by a graph neural network. We introduce a
flexible context aggregation mechanism based on attention, enabling SuperGlue
to reason about the underlying 3D scene and feature assignments jointly.
Compared to traditional, hand-designed heuristics, our technique learns priors
over geometric transformations and regularities of the 3D world through
end-to-end training from image pairs. SuperGlue outperforms other learned
approaches and achieves state-of-the-art results on the task of pose estimation
in challenging real-world indoor and outdoor environments. The proposed method
performs matching in real-time on a modern GPU and can be readily integrated
into modern SfM or SLAM systems. The code and trained weights are publicly
available at https://github.com/magicleap/SuperGluePretrainedNetwork.

keyInsights: Contextual reasoning, specifically via self-attention (intra-image) and cross-attention (inter-image), is crucial for robust feature matching and outlier rejection.

Framing feature matching as a partial assignment problem and solving it with optimal transport (Sinkhorn algorithm) allows for end-to-end learning of matching costs and handling of unmatchable points via a 'dustbin' mechanism.
Integrating 2D keypoint positions with visual descriptors through positional encoding enables the network to leverage crucial spatial information for geometric reasoning.
originalProblem: Traditional feature matching pipelines rely on sequential, decoupled steps with handcrafted heuristics (e.g., Nearest Neighbor search, ratio test, RANSAC) that are often brittle and lack global context.

Prior deep learning approaches to feature matching primarily focused on improving feature quality or post-hoc outlier filtering, failing to address the global assignment structure and contextual reasoning required for robust matching.
The challenge of jointly finding accurate correspondences and robustly rejecting non-matchable keypoints due to occlusion, lack of distinctiveness, or absence in the other image.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance across various geometric computer vision tasks, including 98.3% AUC for DLT-based homography estimation on a synthetic dataset, with matched points being precise enough for non-robust solvers to outperform RANSAC.

Outperformed existing learned and handcrafted methods for indoor (ScanNet, 51.84% AUC@20°) and outdoor (PhotoTourism/MegaDepth, 64.16% AUC@20°) wide-baseline pose estimation, demonstrating significantly higher match precision.
Demonstrated real-time performance, averaging 69 ms (15 FPS) for an indoor image pair with 512 keypoints on a modern GPU, making it suitable for practical applications like SLAM.
solution: Formulates feature matching as a partial assignment problem, which is solved by predicting a soft assignment matrix that includes a 'dustbin' for explicitly handling unmatched keypoints.

Utilizes an Attentional Graph Neural Network (GNN) with alternating self-attention (intra-image) and cross-attention (inter-image) layers to aggregate context and learn enhanced matching descriptors from initial keypoint features and their 2D positions.

Employs an optimal matching layer, which applies the Sinkhorn algorithm to the pairwise similarity scores of the learned descriptors (augmented with dustbin entries) to produce the partial soft assignment matrix.
paper_summary.summary: SuperGlue, developed by researchers including those from Magic Leap, introduces a learnable "middle-end" for feature matching that uses an attentional graph neural network to jointly find correspondences and reject outliers. The method formulates matching as a partial assignment problem, achieving state-of-the-art performance across homography estimation, indoor and outdoor pose estimation, and visual localization benchmarks while operating in real-time.

title: SuperGlue: Learning Feature Matching with Graph Neural Networks

abstract: In recent years, robust matching methods using deep learning-based approaches have been actively studied and improved in computer vision tasks. However, there remains a persistent demand for both robust and fast matching techniques. To address this, we propose a novel Mamba-based local feature matching approach, called MambaGlue, where Mamba is an emerging state-of-the-art architecture rapidly gaining recognition for its superior speed in both training and inference, and promising performance compared with Transformer architectures. In particular, we propose two modules: a) MambaAttention mixer to simultaneously and selectively understand the local and global context through the Mamba-based self-attention structure and b) deep confidence score regressor, which is a multi-layer perceptron (MLP)-based architecture that evaluates a score indicating how confidently matching predictions correspond to the ground-truth correspondences. Consequently, our MambaGlue achieves a balance between robustness and efficiency in real-world applications. As verified on various public datasets, we demonstrate that our MambaGlue yields a substantial performance improvement over baseline approaches while maintaining fast inference speed. Our code will be available on this https URL

keyInsights: Parallel processing of local and global features enables more robust matching
Deep confidence scoring provides more reliable match evaluation than single-layer approaches
Hybrid architectures can effectively combine benefits of different neural network paradigms
originalProblem: Existing feature matching methods face significant trade-offs between accuracy and computational efficiency
Current approaches struggle to effectively balance local and global context understanding in matching tasks
results: Achieved highest precision among sparse matchers in homography estimation
Demonstrated improved accuracy in relative pose estimation with minimal additional processing time
Maintained competitive processing speed while delivering superior performance in outdoor visual localization tasks
Successfully integrated Mamba architecture into practical computer vision applications
solution: Developed MambaAttention mixer that combines Mamba and self-attention mechanisms in parallel
Implemented a sophisticated deep confidence score regressor using MLP architecture
Created an early stopping mechanism to optimize computational resources
paper_summary.summary: KAIST and MIT researchers introduce MambaGlue, a novel feature matching framework that combines Mamba and Transformer architectures to achieve superior accuracy and speed in local feature matching, demonstrating significant improvements in homography estimation and visual localization while maintaining practical computational efficiency.

title: MambaGlue: Fast and Robust Local Feature Matching With Mamba

abstract: The dominant paradigm in image retrieval systems today is to search large databases using global image features, and re-rank those initial results with local image feature matching techniques. This design, dubbed global-to-local, stems from the computational cost of local matching approaches, which can only be afforded for a small number of retrieved images. However, emerging efficient local feature search approaches have opened up new possibilities, in particular enabling detailed retrieval at large scale, to find partial matches which are often missed by global feature search. In parallel, global feature-based re-ranking has shown promising results with high computational efficiency. In this work, we leverage these building blocks to introduce a local-to-global retrieval paradigm, where efficient local feature search meets effective global feature re-ranking. Critically, we propose a re-ranking method where global features are computed on-the-fly, based on the local feature retrieval similarities. Such re-ranking-only global features leverage multidimensional scaling techniques to create embeddings which respect the local similarities obtained during search, enabling a significant re-ranking boost. Experimentally, we demonstrate solid retrieval performance, setting new state-of-the-art results on the Revisited Oxford and Paris datasets.

keyInsights: Reversing the dominant G2L paradigm to L2G, by prioritizing local feature search, significantly improves the ability to find partial and localized matches that global features often miss.
Multidimensional Scaling (MDS), specifically SMACOF, can effectively convert sparse, non-metric local feature similarities into dense, metric global embeddings for robust re-ranking.
Generating these global embeddings on-the-fly per query, from a small set of candidates, makes the MDS re-ranking process computationally efficient and scalable for large datasets.
originalProblem: Traditional "global-to-local" (G2L) image retrieval systems suffer from recall losses during the initial global feature search stage, especially when queries involve only partial matches.
Re-ranking in G2L often operates on individual query-candidate pairs, failing to leverage collective information among shortlisted images to enhance refinement.
Existing re-ranking methods struggle to effectively process non-metric local feature similarities, limiting their utility for detailed localized comparisons.
results: The L2G approach achieved new state-of-the-art mAP scores across ROxf and RPar datasets, including +1M versions (e.g., 79.8% on ROxf+1M Hard, surpassing AMES by 2.1%).
Ablation studies confirmed the crucial role of the MDS-based re-ranking, showing significant performance drops when removed or replaced by methods not suitable for non-metric data.
The framework is modular and generalizable, demonstrating further performance improvements when integrated with different local feature extractors like AMES (e.g., 93.6% on RParis Hard).
solution: A "local-to-global" (L2G) paradigm is proposed, where an initial efficient local feature search, leveraging Constrained Approximate Nearest Neighbors (CANN), retrieves candidates rich in localized similarities.
A novel re-ranking stage employs Multidimensional Scaling (MDS) to generate on-the-fly, query-specific global embeddings from the non-metric local similarities of the initial candidates.
These MDS-derived embeddings are combined with existing global features (e.g., SuperGlobal) and used in a collective re-ranking algorithm to refine retrieval results.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Google and Google DeepMind introduce a "local-to-global" (L2G) image retrieval paradigm that achieves new state-of-the-art performance on standard benchmarks. The method overcomes the limitations of traditional global-to-local approaches, particularly in finding partial matches, by combining efficient local feature search with a novel Multidimensional Scaling (MDS)-based global re-ranking strategy.

title: Global-to-Local or Local-to-Global? Enhancing Image Retrieval with Efficient Local Search and Effective Global Re-ranking

abstract: Feature matching is a challenging computer vision task that involves finding correspondences between two images of a 3D scene. In this paper we consider the dense approach instead of the more common sparse paradigm, thus striving to find all correspondences. Perhaps counter-intuitively, dense methods have previously shown inferior performance to their sparse and semi-sparse counterparts for estimation of two-view geometry. This changes with our novel dense method, which outperforms both dense and sparse methods on geometry estimation. The novelty is threefold: First, we propose a kernel regression global matcher. Secondly, we propose warp refinement through stacked feature maps and depthwise convolution kernels. Thirdly, we propose learning dense confidence through consistent depth and a balanced sampling approach for dense confidence maps. Through extensive experiments we confirm that our proposed dense method, \textbf{D}ense \textbf{K}ernelized Feature \textbf{M}atching, sets a new state-of-the-art on multiple geometry estimation benchmarks. In particular, we achieve an improvement on MegaDepth-1500 of +4.9 and +8.9 AUC$@5^{\circ}$ compared to the best previous sparse method and dense method respectively. Our code is provided at this https URL

keyInsights: Dense feature matching can outperform sparse methods for geometry estimation if the architecture addresses specific shortcomings in global matching, refinement, and certainty.
Framing global matching as a probabilistic regression problem with a Gaussian Process and cosine embeddings effectively captures complex feature relationships and positional information.
Providing a richer input context to warp refiners and using depthwise separable convolutions are critical for accurate high-resolution warp refinement.
originalProblem: Sparse feature matching methods struggle with accurate localization and repeatability in scenes with low texture or large viewpoint changes.
Prior dense matching methods, despite offering complete pixel correspondences, consistently underperformed sparse methods for two-view geometry estimation.
The fundamental components of existing dense matching architectures, including global matching, warp refinement, and certainty estimation, were suboptimal.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on HPatches Homography (71.3 AUC@3px) and MegaDepth-1500 Pose Estimation (60.4 AUC@5°), surpassing top sparse methods.
Set new benchmarks on challenging indoor scenes like ScanNet-1500 (29.4 AUC@5°), outperforming previous best sparse and dense methods.
Ablation studies confirmed the significant contributions of kernelized regression, enhanced warp refiners (+4.8 AUC@5°), and balanced match sampling (+2.0 AUC@5°).
solution: A kernel regression global matcher formulates matching as an embedded probabilistic regression using a Gaussian Process with cosine coordinate embeddings.
Enhanced warp refiners leverage richer input representations and larger 5x5 depthwise separable convolution kernels for progressive refinement.
A robust certainty estimation method learns to predict match reliability based on depth consistency, combined with a balanced sampling strategy for diverse match selection.
paper_summary.summary: DKM introduces a novel dense kernelized feature matching approach that significantly outperforms both sparse and prior dense methods for two-view geometry estimation. It achieved a 71.3 AUC@3px on HPatches Homography and 60.4 AUC@5° on MegaDepth-1500 pose estimation, setting new state-of-the-art benchmarks across various datasets.

title: DKM: Dense Kernelized Feature Matching for Geometry Estimation

abstract: Local feature matching between images remains a challenging task, especially in the presence of significant appearance variations, e.g., extreme viewpoint changes. In this work, we propose DeepMatcher, a deep Transformer-based network built upon our investigation of local feature matching in detector-free methods. The key insight is that local feature matcher with deep layers can capture more human-intuitive and simpler-to-match features. Based on this, we propose a Slimming Transformer (SlimFormer) dedicated for DeepMatcher, which leverages vector-based attention to model relevance among all keypoints and achieves long-range context aggregation in an efficient and effective manner. A relative position encoding is applied to each SlimFormer so as to explicitly disclose relative distance information, further improving the representation of keypoints. A layer-scale strategy is also employed in each SlimFormer to enable the network to assimilate message exchange from the residual block adaptively, thus allowing it to simulate the human behaviour that humans can acquire different matching cues each time they scan an image pair. To facilitate a better adaption of the SlimFormer, we introduce a Feature Transition Module (FTM) to ensure a smooth transition in feature scopes with different receptive fields. By interleaving the self- and cross-SlimFormer multiple times, DeepMatcher can easily establish pixel-wise dense matches at coarse level. Finally, we perceive the match refinement as a combination of classification and regression problems and design Fine Matches Module to predict confidence and offset concurrently, thereby generating robust and accurate matches. Experimentally, we show that DeepMatcher significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art methods on several benchmarks, demonstrating the superior matching capability of DeepMatcher.

keyInsights: The Feature Transition Module effectively bridges the context gap between local CNN features and global transformer layers, enhancing feature representation.

The Slimming Transformer's vector-based attention, relative position encoding, and layer-scale strategy allow for the effective use of deeper networks, overcoming performance plateaus seen in prior transformer-based methods.
A two-stage coarse-to-fine matching process, combining global correlation with local refinement, contributes to both high recall and precision in correspondences.
originalProblem: Traditional detector-based feature matching struggles with viewpoint changes and blurry images.
Existing detector-free methods are computationally expensive and suffer from a 'context range gap' between CNN local features and transformer global contexts.
Previous deep feature matching networks exhibit limited performance gains with increasing transformer depth due to issues like translation invariance ambiguity and suboptimal network design.
results: DeepMatcher achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmark datasets including ScanNet, MegaDepth, and HPatches, outperforming existing methods like LoFTR and SuperGlue.
The model consistently finds more correct matches with higher precision in challenging indoor and outdoor scenes, including those with low texture or repetitive patterns.

Ablation studies confirm the contribution of each component: the Feature Transition Module improves performance by 1.5-2.0%, relative position encoding by 2.5-3.0%, and the Fine Matches Module by 1.0-1.5%.
solution: DeepMatcher employs a transformer-based architecture featuring a CNN-based local feature extractor and a two-stage coarse-to-fine matching process.
A novel Feature Transition Module (FTM) uses multi-scale convolutions to bridge the context between CNN features and transformer layers.
The Slimming Transformer (SlimFormer) incorporates vector-based attention, relative position encoding (RoPE), and a layer-scale strategy for efficient and effective long-range context aggregation.
paper_summary.summary: DeepMatcher, developed by researchers from Harbin Institute of Technology and SenseTime Group Inc., introduces a transformer-based architecture for local feature matching that achieves state-of-the-art performance on benchmarks like ScanNet, MegaDepth, and HPatches while maintaining computational efficiency. It addresses limitations of prior detector-free methods by bridging the CNN-transformer context gap and enabling deeper transformer architectures.

title: DeepMatcher: A Deep Transformer-based Network for Robust and Accurate Local Feature Matching

abstract: In this work we address the problem of finding reliable pixel-level correspondences under difficult imaging conditions. We propose an approach where a single convolutional neural network plays a dual role: It is simultaneously a dense feature descriptor and a feature detector. By postponing the detection to a later stage, the obtained keypoints are more stable than their traditional counterparts based on early detection of low-level structures. We show that this model can be trained using pixel correspondences extracted from readily available large-scale SfM reconstructions, without any further annotations. The proposed method obtains state-of-the-art performance on both the difficult Aachen Day-Night localization dataset and the InLoc indoor localization benchmark, as well as competitive performance on other benchmarks for image matching and 3D reconstruction.
title: D2-Net: A Trainable CNN for Joint Detection and Description of Local Features

abstract:
Matching visible and near-infrared (NIR) images remains a significant
challenge in remote sensing image fusion. The nonlinear radiometric differences
between heterogeneous remote sensing images make the image matching task even
more difficult. Deep learning has gained substantial attention in computer
vision tasks in recent years. However, many methods rely on supervised learning
and necessitate large amounts of annotated data. Nevertheless, annotated data
is frequently limited in the field of remote sensing image matching. To address
this challenge, this paper proposes a novel keypoint descriptor approach that
obtains robust feature descriptors via a self-supervised matching network. A
light-weight transformer network, termed as LTFormer, is designed to generate
deep-level feature descriptors. Furthermore, we implement an innovative triplet
loss function, LT Loss, to enhance the matching performance further. Our
approach outperforms conventional hand-crafted local feature descriptors and
proves equally competitive compared to state-of-the-art deep learning-based
methods, even amidst the shortage of annotated data.

organization_info.name: Nanjing University of Science and Technology

title: A Light-weight Transformer-based Self-supervised Matching Network for  Heterogeneous Images

abstract:
Semantic correspondence made tremendous progress through the recent
advancements of large vision models (LVM). While these LVMs have been shown to
reliably capture local semantics, the same can currently not be said for
capturing global geometric relationships between semantic object regions. This
problem leads to unreliable performance for semantic correspondence between
images with extreme view variation. In this work, we aim to leverage monocular
depth estimates to capture these geometric relationships for more robust and
data-efficient semantic correspondence. First, we introduce a simple but
effective method to build 3D object-class representations from monocular depth
estimates and LVM features using a sparsely annotated image correspondence
dataset. Second, we formulate an alignment energy that can be minimized using
gradient descent to obtain an alignment between the 3D object-class
representation and the object-class instance in the input RGB-image. Our method
achieves state-of-the-art matching accuracy in multiple categories on the
challenging SPair-71k dataset, increasing the PCK@0.1 score by more than 10
points on three categories and overall by 3.3 points from 85.6% to 88.9%.
Additional resources and code are available at this https URL
title: SemAlign3D: Semantic Correspondence between RGB-Images through Aligning  3D Object-Class Representations

abstract:
Recent feature matching methods have achieved remarkable performance but lack
efficiency consideration. In this paper, we revisit the mainstream
detector-free matching pipeline and improve all its stages considering both
accuracy and efficiency. We propose an Efficient Deep feature Matching network,
EDM. We first adopt a deeper CNN with fewer dimensions to extract multi-level
features. Then we present a Correlation Injection Module that conducts feature
transformation on high-level deep features, and progressively injects feature
correlations from global to local for efficient multi-scale feature
aggregation, improving both speed and performance. In the refinement stage, a
novel lightweight bidirectional axis-based regression head is designed to
directly predict subpixel-level correspondences from latent features, avoiding
the significant computational cost of explicitly locating keypoints on
high-resolution local feature heatmaps. Moreover, effective selection
strategies are introduced to enhance matching accuracy. Extensive experiments
show that our EDM achieves competitive matching accuracy on various benchmarks
and exhibits excellent efficiency, offering valuable best practices for
real-world applications. The code is available at
this https URL
title: EDM: Efficient Deep Feature Matching

abstract: We present a novel method for efficiently producing semi-dense matches across images. Previous detector-free matcher LoFTR has shown remarkable matching capability in handling large-viewpoint change and texture-poor scenarios but suffers from low efficiency. We revisit its design choices and derive multiple improvements for both efficiency and accuracy. One key observation is that performing the transformer over the entire feature map is redundant due to shared local information, therefore we propose an aggregated attention mechanism with adaptive token selection for efficiency. Furthermore, we find spatial variance exists in LoFTR's fine correlation module, which is adverse to matching accuracy. A novel two-stage correlation layer is proposed to achieve accurate subpixel correspondences for accuracy improvement. Our efficiency optimized model is $\sim 2.5\times$ faster than LoFTR which can even surpass state-of-the-art efficient sparse matching pipeline SuperPoint + LightGlue. Moreover, extensive experiments show that our method can achieve higher accuracy compared with competitive semi-dense matchers, with considerable efficiency benefits. This opens up exciting prospects for large-scale or latency-sensitive applications such as image retrieval and 3D reconstruction. Project page: this https URL.

keyInsights: Dense global attention in transformer-based matchers like LoFTR often contains redundant computations, and adaptive token aggregation can significantly reduce computational cost while improving accuracy.

The dual-softmax operator, while crucial for training discriminative features, can be efficiently bypassed during inference for coarse matching, yielding substantial speedups with minimal accuracy drop.

Subpixel refinement can be made more accurate and less prone to spatial variance by employing a two-stage approach: first obtaining a robust pixel-level match, then performing highly localized subpixel estimation within a small window.
originalProblem: Previous detector-free semi-dense matching methods, particularly LoFTR, were computationally inefficient due to dense transformer operations on large feature maps, hindering their use in practical, large-scale applications.
The fine correlation module in LoFTR suffered from spatial variance, which adversely affected the accuracy of subpixel correspondences.
Achieving a balance between the robustness and density of detector-free methods and the speed of traditional sparse methods remained a challenge.
results: Achieves 27.0 ms per image pair on ScanNet (640x480 resolution), making it faster than LoFTR (66.2 ms) and even surpassing the sparse matcher SuperPoint + LightGlue (30.7 ms).
Attains 56.4 AUC@5° on MegaDepth for relative pose estimation, outperforming LoFTR (52.8) and demonstrating improved accuracy alongside speed.
Shows strong performance in homography estimation on HPatches (66.5 AUC@3px) and competitive results in visual localization on InLoc and Aachen v1.1 datasets.
solution: Employs a lightweight RepVGG backbone for efficient multi-resolution feature extraction and introduces an Aggregated Attention module to reduce transformer computational cost by adaptively aggregating tokens.
Optimizes the coarse-level matching by demonstrating and leveraging the dispensability of the dual-softmax operator during inference for significant speed gains.

Proposes a two-stage correlation method for subpixel refinement, performing an initial pixel-level matching via MNN on local patches, followed by a localized expectation calculation for subpixel accuracy, reducing spatial variance.
paper_summary.summary: Efficient LoFTR introduces a semi-dense local feature matching method that achieves speeds comparable to sparse methods while maintaining or improving accuracy over its predecessor, LoFTR. This is accomplished through architectural optimizations including an aggregated attention module and a two-stage subpixel refinement, enabling robust correspondence estimation for real-time applications.

title: Efficient LoFTR: Semi-Dense Local Feature Matching with Sparse-Like Speed

abstract: Methods that combine local and global features have recently shown excellent performance on multiple challenging deep image retrieval benchmarks, but their use of local features raises at least two issues. First, these local features simply boil down to the localized map activations of a neural network, and hence can be extremely redundant. Second, they are typically trained with a global loss that only acts on top of an aggregation of local features; by contrast, testing is based on local feature matching, which creates a discrepancy between training and testing. In this paper, we propose a novel architecture for deep image retrieval, based solely on mid-level features that we call Super-features. These Super-features are constructed by an iterative attention module and constitute an ordered set in which each element focuses on a localized and discriminant image pattern. For training, they require only image labels. A contrastive loss operates directly at the level of Super-features and focuses on those that match across images. A second complementary loss encourages diversity. Experiments on common landmark retrieval benchmarks validate that Super-features substantially outperform state-of-the-art methods when using the same number of features, and only require a significantly smaller memory footprint to match their performance. Code and models are available at: this https URL.

keyInsights: Directly supervising mid-level attentional features with a combination of contrastive and decorrelation losses effectively learns discriminant and non-redundant representations without requiring pixel-level annotations.

Leveraging an ordered set of Super-features, via learned templates and a 'same Super-feature ID' matching constraint, provides a robust mechanism for establishing fine-grained pseudo-ground truth during training.

Aligning the training objective directly with the inference mechanism (using Super-features for both) resolves the training-testing discrepancy, leading to improved retrieval performance and efficiency.
originalProblem: Existing CNN-based local features for image retrieval are often highly redundant, leading to inefficient representations and high memory consumption.
A discrepancy exists between training (global loss on aggregated features) and testing (direct local feature matching), which limits the discriminative power of features for fine-grained tasks.

Current mid-level attentional features either lack sufficient localization or produce too few features, and global supervision often fails to make them discriminative enough for instance-level recognition.
results: FIRe achieves state-of-the-art performance on ROxford (81.8 mAP med, 61.2 mAP hard) and RParis (85.3 mAP med, 70.0 mAP hard), surpassing previous methods like HOW by up to 5.2 mAP.
The method significantly reduces memory footprint, requiring approximately four times less memory (e.g., 383 ASMK clusters per image vs. 469 for HOW on R1M) for comparable or superior performance.

Ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of the 'same Super-feature ID' constraint in selecting high-quality matches and the attention decorrelation loss in reducing feature redundancy and improving performance by over 1%.
solution: Proposes 'Super-features', a novel image representation based on localized and discriminant mid-level attentional features generated by an iterative Local feature Integration Transformer (LIT) module.

Develops a training framework that applies direct supervision at the Super-feature level using only image-level labels, combining a contrastive loss for matching Super-features and an attention decorrelation loss to promote diversity.
Establishes Super-feature correspondences for training using reciprocal nearest neighbors, Lowe's ratio test, and a 'same Super-feature ID' constraint derived from learned templates.
paper_summary.summary: NAVER LABS Europe introduces Feature Integration-based Retrieval (FIRe), a framework that employs novel 'Super-features'—mid-level attentional features learned by an iterative Local feature Integration Transformer (LIT)—as the sole image representation for retrieval. This approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on landmark retrieval benchmarks such as ROxford and RParis, demonstrating up to 5.2 mAP improvement while reducing memory footprint by approximately four times compared to existing methods.

title: Learning Super-Features for Image Retrieval

abstract: Finding local features that are repeatable across multiple views is a cornerstone of sparse 3D reconstruction. The classical image matching paradigm detects keypoints per-image once and for all, which can yield poorly-localized features and propagate large errors to the final geometry. In this paper, we refine two key steps of structure-from-motion by a direct alignment of low-level image information from multiple views: we first adjust the initial keypoint locations prior to any geometric estimation, and subsequently refine points and camera poses as a post-processing. This refinement is robust to large detection noise and appearance changes, as it optimizes a featuremetric error based on dense features predicted by a neural network. This significantly improves the accuracy of camera poses and scene geometry for a wide range of keypoint detectors, challenging viewing conditions, and off-the-shelf deep features. Our system easily scales to large image collections, enabling pixel-perfect crowd-sourced localization at scale. Our code is publicly available at this https URL as an add-on to the popular SfM software COLMAP.

keyInsights: Locally dense, learned features provide superior robustness and fine-grained subpixel accuracy for alignment compared to raw pixel intensities or purely geometric errors.
Combining pre-SfM keypoint refinement (F-KA) with post-SfM global bundle adjustment (F-BA) using featuremetric optimization is crucial for achieving end-to-end 'pixel-perfect' 3D reconstructions.

Careful memory optimization strategies, like approximating dense feature residuals, make featuremetric bundle adjustment computationally practical and scalable for datasets containing thousands of images.
originalProblem: Traditional sparse Structure-from-Motion (SfM) pipelines suffer from inherent inaccuracies in 2D keypoint localization, which propagate through the entire reconstruction process.
Keypoints are typically detected independently in each image, leading to sub-optimal precision and quantization errors that limit the accuracy of estimated 3D points and camera poses.

Existing refinement methods, such as standard Bundle Adjustment (BA) or 'Patch Flow', are limited in fully mitigating these errors due to their reliance on purely geometric observations or approximate flow fields.
results: Achieved significantly more accurate and complete 3D point clouds on the ETH3D benchmark, increasing 1cm accuracy for SuperPoint features on indoor scenes from 75.76% (unrefined) to 89.33%.
Improved camera pose estimation accuracy, with the Area Under the Curve (AUC) for translation error up to 1cm increasing from 51.20% to 71.97% for SuperPoint features.
The refinement method is an order of magnitude faster than prior dense refinement techniques ('Patch Flow') and demonstrates practical scalability by refining a 7k-image model in less than 2 hours.
solution: Introduces 'featuremetric refinement,' which leverages direct image alignment in a learned, dense feature space to achieve subpixel accuracy for local image details.

Integrates this refinement into two key stages: Featuremetric Keypoint Adjustment (F-KA) to refine 2D keypoint locations before 3D estimation, and Featuremetric Bundle Adjustment (F-BA) to refine global 3D structure and camera poses after initial SfM.
Employs memory-efficient techniques, such as precomputing feature distance patches, and robust optimization with Levenberg-Marquardt to ensure scalability to large image collections.
paper_summary.summary: The paper enhances Structure-from-Motion accuracy by employing featuremetric refinement with dense, learned features at two critical stages: initial keypoint adjustment and post-reconstruction bundle adjustment. This method yields more precise 2D keypoint locations, 3D scene geometry, and camera poses while remaining scalable for large datasets.

title: Pixel-Perfect Structure-from-Motion with Featuremetric Refinement

abstract: Local feature matching remains a fundamental challenge in computer vision. Recent Area to Point Matching (A2PM) methods have improved matching accuracy. However, existing research based on this framework relies on inefficient pixel-level comparisons and complex graph matching that limit scalability. In this work, we introduce the Semantic and Geometric-aware Descriptor Network (SGAD), which fundamentally rethinks area-based matching by generating highly discriminative area descriptors that enable direct matching without complex graph optimization. This approach significantly improves both accuracy and efficiency of area matching. We further improve the performance of area matching through a novel supervision strategy that decomposes the area matching task into classification and ranking subtasks. Finally, we introduce the Hierarchical Containment Redundancy Filter (HCRF) to eliminate overlapping areas by analyzing containment graphs. SGAD demonstrates remarkable performance gains, reducing runtime by 60x (0.82s vs. 60.23s) compared to MESA. Extensive evaluations show consistent improvements across multiple point matchers: SGAD+LoFTR reduces runtime compared to DKM, while achieving higher accuracy (0.82s vs. 1.51s, 65.98 vs. 61.11) in outdoor pose estimation, and SGAD+ROMA delivers +7.39% AUC@5° in indoor pose estimation, establishing a new state-of-the-art.

keyInsights: Generating strong, holistic area descriptors that integrate both semantic and geometric information allows for direct and efficient area matching using a simple descriptor matcher, bypassing complex graph optimization or pixel-level comparisons.
Explicitly modeling spatial relationships between areas within an image through geometric positional encoding significantly enhances the distinctiveness and contextual awareness of area descriptors.

A two-stage supervision approach (classification for absolute similarity and ranking for relative similarity) combined with post-matching hierarchical redundancy filtering leads to more accurate and efficient area correspondences.
originalProblem: Existing Area to Point Matching (A2PM) methods, like MESA and DMESA, suffered from inefficient pixel-level comparisons and complex graph matching for area correspondences, leading to high computational costs.

Previous A2PM approaches struggled with inconsistent or inaccurate area matches due to limitations in their area representations and merging strategies, hindering downstream pixel-level matching accuracy.
Developing a feature matching framework robust to challenging conditions (viewpoint changes, untextured scenes) while maintaining efficiency and scalability remained a core challenge.
results: SGAD achieves state-of-the-art area matching performance with AUC scores consistently exceeding 95% on ScanNet and MegaDepth datasets.

The method significantly boosts relative pose estimation accuracy, with SGAD+LoFTR showing a +233.22% improvement in AUC@5° over standalone LoFTR on ScanNet (1296x968) and SGAD+ROMA setting new state-of-the-art benchmarks.

SGAD offers substantial computational efficiency, processing image pairs up to 60 times faster than MESA (e.g., 0.82s vs. 60.23s for SGAD+LoFTR vs. MESA+LoFTR on MegaDepth), while also outperforming denser, slower methods like DKM in both speed and accuracy.
solution: SGAD develops an Area Description Network that leverages a DINOv2 backbone for semantic features, enriched by a novel geometric positional encoding, and refined through attention mechanisms to generate compact, highly discriminative area descriptors.

A Hierarchical Containment Redundancy Filter (HCRF) is introduced to effectively prune redundant or extensively overlapping matched areas, optimizing subsequent pixel-level matching without sacrificing viable correspondences.

A dual-task supervision strategy, combining classification and ranking losses, is used to train the area descriptor matcher, enabling it to learn both absolute and relative similarity patterns for robust area correspondence.
paper_summary.summary: SGAD introduces a semantic and geometric-aware descriptor for local feature matching, achieving efficient and accurate area-to-point matching by generating highly discriminative area descriptors and employing a hierarchical filtering mechanism. The method significantly improves the accuracy and speed of relative pose estimation, achieving state-of-the-art performance when integrated with various point matchers.

title: SGAD: Semantic and Geometric-aware Descriptor for Local Feature Matching

abstract:
Accurate and robust correspondence matching is of utmost importance for
various 3D computer vision tasks. However, traditional explicit
programming-based methods often struggle to handle challenging scenarios, and
deep learning-based methods require large well-labeled datasets for network
training. In this article, we introduce Epipolar-Constrained Cascade
Correspondence (E3CM), a novel approach that addresses these limitations.
Unlike traditional methods, E3CM leverages pre-trained convolutional neural
networks to match correspondence, without requiring annotated data for any
network training or fine-tuning. Our method utilizes epipolar constraints to
guide the matching process and incorporates a cascade structure for progressive
refinement of matches. We extensively evaluate the performance of E3CM through
comprehensive experiments and demonstrate its superiority over existing
methods. To promote further research and facilitate reproducibility, we make
our source code publicly available at this https URL
title: E3CM: Epipolar-Constrained Cascade Correspondence Matching

abstract: We propose a new dataset for learning local image descriptors which can be used for significantly improved patch matching. Our proposed dataset consists of an order of magnitude more number of scenes, images, and positive and negative correspondences compared to the currently available Multi-View Stereo (MVS) dataset from Brown et al. The new dataset also has better coverage of the overall viewpoint, scale, and lighting changes in comparison to the MVS dataset. Our dataset also provides supplementary information like RGB patches with scale and rotations values, and intrinsic and extrinsic camera parameters which as shown later can be used to customize training data as per application. We train an existing state-of-the-art model on our dataset and evaluate on publicly available benchmarks such as HPatches dataset and Strecha et al.\cite{strecha} to quantify the image descriptor performance. Experimental evaluations show that the descriptors trained using our proposed dataset outperform the current state-of-the-art descriptors trained on MVS by 8%, 4% and 10% on matching, verification and retrieval tasks respectively on the HPatches dataset. Similarly on the Strecha dataset, we see an improvement of 3-5% for the matching task in non-planar scenes.
title: A Large Dataset for Improving Patch Matching

abstract: As a core step in structure-from-motion and SLAM, robust feature detection and description under challenging scenarios such as significant viewpoint changes remain unresolved despite their ubiquity. While recent works have identified the importance of local features in modeling geometric transformations, these methods fail to learn the visual cues present in long-range relationships. We present Robust Deformable Detector (RDD), a novel and robust keypoint detector/descriptor leveraging the deformable transformer, which captures global context and geometric invariance through deformable self-attention mechanisms. Specifically, we observed that deformable attention focuses on key locations, effectively reducing the search space complexity and modeling the geometric invariance.
